title: What the War Left Open
bullets: The Civil War of 1861 to 1865 killed somewhere around 750,000 people, more Americans than died in the Revolution, the War of 1812, the Mexican War, both World Wars, Korea, and Vietnam combined.
The war answered one question with total finality and sealed it shut with the Thirteenth Amendment: the United States would be free, not slave.
It did not answer the next question, the one this whole book is about: four million freed people were now free, but did free mean only not-owned, or did it mean a citizen with the vote?
Roughly two-thirds of Southern wealth had evaporated because emancipation alone wiped out the single largest category of property in the region.
The first acts of freedom were heartbreakingly concrete: walking sometimes hundreds of miles to look for a wife or a child sold away years before, and lining up to legally marry.
Reconstruction was the first serious attempt at a multiracial democracy in a major nation, with the closest structural rehearsal being the British Caribbean's compulsory apprenticeship after 1834.
The Dunning School taught for fifty years that Reconstruction was Negro misrule looted by carpetbaggers and scalawags, an account constructed after the fact by the side that overthrew Reconstruction.
The fight had four sides: the president Andrew Johnson, the Radical Republicans led by Thaddeus Stevens and Charles Sumner, the freedpeople themselves, and the ex-Confederates and their allies.

title: The Accidental President
bullets: Five days after Robert E. Lee surrendered at Appomattox, President Abraham Lincoln was shot at Ford's Theatre by John Wilkes Booth, the first assassination of an American president.
Eight conspirators were tried by a military tribunal and four were hanged that summer, including Mary Surratt, the first woman executed by the federal government.
In his last public speech Lincoln had endorsed voting rights for some Black men, the first time any American president publicly backed Black suffrage at all.
Andrew Johnson was a self-made Tennessee Democrat who hated the planter class out of pure resentment but was himself a committed, explicit white supremacist who had owned enslaved people.
With Congress out of session until December, for eight months Reconstruction simply was whatever Andrew Johnson decided it was, a phase historians call Presidential Reconstruction.
Johnson's amnesty and pardon proclamation restored land and political rights to ex-Confederate planters by the thousands, often the same day they came to grovel.

His restoration of state governments demanded almost nothing of the freedpeople and sent a Congress full of ex-Confederates to Washington, including Confederate vice president Alexander Stephens of the Cornerstone Speech.
By autumn 1865 a Northern public that had buried 360,000 soldiers saw the South losing the war and winning the peace, with the active help of the president.

title: Slavery by Another Name
bullets: The Black Codes, starting in Mississippi and South Carolina in late 1865, were laws that applied only to Black people and recreated the labor system of slavery without the ownership.
Vagrancy laws let any Black person without proof of employment be arrested, fined, and hired out to a white employer, almost exactly the South African pass law later built into apartheid.
The Freedmen's Bureau, run by Union general Oliver Otis Howard, was handed one of the largest social tasks in the country's history and the resources of a rounding error.
Sherman's Special Field Order No. 15 had set aside coastal land in 40-acre plots, the literal origin of forty acres and a mule, with some 40,000 freedpeople farming 400,000 acres by mid-1865.
Andrew Johnson gave the land back to its former Confederate owners, and Howard was sent in person to Edisto Island to tell freed families to their faces.
Thaddeus Stevens proposed seizing the great plantations outright, but Congress, unwilling to attack property rights even of traitors, refused.
Sharecropping let a family keep a share of the crop instead of wages, but the crop-lien chained it to a merchant at effective interest commonly in the 40 to 60 percent range.
The merchant kept the only ledger, so an enormous share of croppers were simply told the year's labor had not cleared the year's debt and entered the next year already owing.

title: The Break
bullets: When Congress reconvened in December 1865 it refused to seat the ex-Confederate senators and representatives Johnson's process had sent, the procedural hinge of everything that followed.
The Civil Rights Act of 1866 declared all persons born in the United States citizens with equal rights, a federal sledgehammer aimed directly at the Black Codes.
Johnson vetoed both the Freedmen's Bureau and Civil Rights bills, arguing they discriminated in favor of Black Americans against whites, language a modern reader will find eerily familiar.
In April 1866 Congress overrode the veto of a major piece of legislation for the first time in American history, marking the moment power over Reconstruction passed to the legislature.
In the Memphis massacre a white mob with police and firemen in it killed about 46 Black residents over three days; the New Orleans massacre killed roughly 34 to 48 more.
Johnson's Swing Around the Circle saw him trade insults with hecklers and compare his persecution to Christ's, degrading the dignity of the presidency itself.
The November 1866 elections produced a Republican landslide giving the party more than two-thirds of both houses, meaning Congress could override any veto at will.
The president was now, on Reconstruction, a spectator, and the question became what Congress would do with near-total power.

title: The Second Founding
bullets: Between 1865 and 1870 three amendments changed what citizenship is and whose side the federal government is on, what modern historians call the Second Founding.
The Thirteenth Amendment banned slavery except as a punishment for crime, a loophole the South drove convict leasing through, conditions frequently deadlier than slavery.
The Fourteenth Amendment's first section did four things: the Citizenship Clause overturning Dred Scott, the Privileges or Immunities Clause, the Due Process Clause, and the Equal Protection Clause.
For the first time the Fourteenth Amendment made the states answerable to the federal Constitution for how they treat the people inside them.
The amendment was a moderate-Radical bargain largely drafted by moderate Republican John Bingham of Ohio, not a Radical dictation, which is part of why it had gaps.
Every Southern state under Johnson's governments except Tennessee rejected the Fourteenth Amendment when offered it as the price of readmission.
The Reconstruction Acts of March 1867 dissolved the Southern governments and divided ten states into five military districts under U.S. Army generals.
Army-appointed boards signed up roughly 700,000 Black and 660,000 white voters, and men who two years earlier had been legally property sat as elected delegates writing new state constitutions.

title: The Trial of a President
bullets: In spring 1868 the United States put the president on trial to remove him for the first time, and would not do it again for 130 years.
Impeachment is not removal: the House impeaches by simple majority, the Senate convicts by two-thirds, so in 1868 conviction required 36 of 54 senators.
Congress passed the Tenure of Office Act in 1867, a trap of dubious constitutionality designed so that if Johnson fired Secretary of War Edwin Stanton he could be impeached.
Johnson sprang the trap on purpose in February 1868, dismissing Stanton, who barricaded himself in the War Department, and the House impeached Johnson 126 to 47.
The formal charge was thin, but the real reason was that Johnson was using every power of the presidency to sabotage Reconstruction and protect the ex-Confederate order.
The Senate voted 35 to convict, 19 to acquit, one vote short of the 36 needed, with seven Republicans breaking ranks, the most remembered being Edmund Ross of Kansas.
Ross was mythologized in Profiles in Courage but later historians documented federal patronage he sought and won around the time of his vote, so the heroism is debated.
The acquittal established the durable norm that a president will not be removed merely for pursuing policies Congress hates, only for something broadly accepted as a high crime.

title: Black Reconstruction
bullets: In 1860 it was a crime in most of the South to teach an enslaved person to read; by 1867 the same people were registering voters by the hundreds of thousands.
The Union League turned Black churches and schoolhouses into political clubs producing a disciplined, almost unanimously Republican Black electorate.
Around 2,000 Black men held public office, sixteen sat in Congress, and roughly 600 Black men served in Southern state legislatures.
Hiram Rhodes Revels took his seat in February 1870, the first Black member of Congress, filling a Mississippi Senate seat once held by Confederate president Jefferson Davis.
Blanche K. Bruce, born enslaved, took the other Mississippi Senate seat in 1875; Joseph Rainey of South Carolina became the first Black representative.
Robert Smalls had pulled off one of the war's most audacious escapes, sailing the Confederate steamer Planter past the harbor forts and delivering it to the Union navy.
No Black American won a Senate election anywhere until Edward Brooke of Massachusetts in 1966, ninety-one years after Bruce, the measure of how complete the defeat was.

Freedpeople founded Howard University, Fisk University, Hampton Institute, and Morehouse College, the historically Black colleges that would educate the Black professional class for the Jim Crow century.
The Fisk Jubilee Singers toured the North and then Britain in 1873, performing for Queen Victoria and carrying the spirituals composed in slavery into the world's musical canon.

title: Forty Acres, and What Replaced It
bullets: The formerly enslaved were freed into propertylessness in a place where the only people with property were the people who had enslaved them.
Thaddeus Stevens proposed confiscating the large plantations of leading Confederates and distributing the land to freedpeople in 40-acre allotments.
The plan was killed not mainly by Southerners but by Northern Republicans who found wholesale land redistribution a more frightening precedent than they could accept.
The same Congress that would not give freedpeople 40 acres of traitors' land was handing railroad corporations tens of millions of acres of public land.
The Southern Homestead Act of 1866 was a fig leaf: the land was largely pine barren and swamp and the act was a near-total failure, repealed in 1876.
In 1880, fifteen years after emancipation, the great majority of Black Southern farm families still owned no land at all and worked someone else's.
The crop-lien locked the whole region into cotton while the North mechanized, and the South fell behind for decades as a structural consequence of how emancipation was financed.
The Freedman's Savings and Trust Company failed in 1874 after its white trustees steered deposits into reckless self-dealing loans, souring Black engagement with American banking for generations.

title: The Grant Years
bullets: Ulysses S. Grant won the 1868 election on the slogan Let us have peace, and the roughly 500,000 newly enfranchised Black men were plausibly the margin of his victory.
After 1868 both parties understood that the fight over Black voting was, among other things, a fight over who controls the United States.
The Fifteenth Amendment, ratified February 1870, forbade denying the vote on grounds of race, color, or previous condition of servitude.
Its fatal flaw was its wording: it is silent on poll taxes, literacy tests, and property requirements, a negative prohibition with a hole the size of Jim Crow in it.
The amendment split the women's suffrage cause, with Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony opposing it while Lucy Stone backed it, the movement breaking for two decades.
The transcontinental railroad was completed with a golden spike at Promontory Summit in May 1869, even as the South's future was being fought over.
Grant ran two campaigns with one small army: conquest against Native nations in the West, resourced relentlessly, and protection of Black citizens in the South, resourced grudgingly.
The Black Friday gold-corner scheme of 1869 began a wave of scandals; Grant himself was never shown to have stolen anything but his enemies got the argument that his party was rotten.

title: The Klan Wars
bullets: The destruction of Reconstruction was in significant and documented part a sustained campaign of murder, arson, rape, and assassination, organized political terrorism.
The Ku Klux Klan began in Pulaski, Tennessee, as a social club of bored young Confederate veterans and within two years metastasized into a decentralized terror network.
Its probable first national leader was Nathan Bedford Forrest, a prewar slave trader and the officer in command at the 1864 Fort Pillow massacre of surrendering Black soldiers.
A 1871 joint committee of Congress published a thirteen-volume report on Klan violence, one of the fullest first-person records of organized terror anywhere in history.
Congress passed three Enforcement Acts, the strongest being the Ku Klux Klan Act of April 1871, which let the president use the army and suspend habeas corpus.
Congress created the Department of Justice in 1870 in significant part to give Washington the lawyers and marshals it needed to prosecute the Klan.
In October 1871 Grant suspended habeas corpus in nine South Carolina counties, federal prosecutors won hundreds of convictions, and the first Klan was broken by 1872.
The 1871 Klan trials are the clean experiment proving the terror was solvable: where federal enforcement was energetic the violence fell and Reconstruction functioned.

title: The North Loses Interest
bullets: Reconstruction was abandoned, not lost: by 1872 the federal government had the legal tools, the proven method, and a recent success, and lost only the will.
The Crédit Mobilier scandal, the Whiskey Ring, and the Belknap affair made the party of Lincoln nationally synonymous with graft and drained its moral authority.
In 1872 reform-minded Liberal Republicans bolted, announcing that a respectable slice of the North considered Reconstruction a tiresome project the country should wind down.
The 1872 Amnesty Act restored political rights to most of the roughly 150,000 ex-Confederates still barred, a gift to the counter-revolution timed almost perfectly.
The crash that began in Vienna reached America when Jay Cooke and Company failed in September 1873, setting off the Panic of 1873 and the Long Depression.
A ruined Northern voter in 1874 cared about the money question, greenbacks versus the gold standard, far more than about Black Southerners a thousand miles away.
In the 1874 midterms the Democrats captured the House of Representatives for the first time since before the war, ending Congressional Reconstruction as a legislative engine.
After 1874 the federal government still had the laws on the books; it simply stopped funding and using them, and the men afraid to ride in 1872 understood no one was coming.

title: The Supreme Court Disarms the Constitution
bullets: Between 1873 and 1876 the Supreme Court read the three amendments of the Second Founding so narrowly that the laws meant to enforce them were left nearly powerless.
The Slaughter-House Cases of 1873 read the Privileges or Immunities Clause out of the Constitution five years after ratification, in a case the public barely noticed because it was about meatpacking.
In Bradwell v. Illinois the Court upheld refusing to license Myra Bradwell to practice law because she was a woman, with a concurrence on woman's divinely designed destiny.
In Minor v. Happersett the Court ruled the Fourteenth Amendment's citizenship did not include a right to vote, so states could constitutionally deny women the ballot.
United States v. Cruikshank held the Fourteenth Amendment restrains only states, not private mobs, so the federal government could not prosecute the Colfax massacre's killers.
United States v. Reese struck down the Enforcement Act sections and held the Fifteenth Amendment did not confer a right to vote, leaving poll taxes and literacy tests untouched.
The Court informed every white paramilitary that a sufficiently private massacre was beyond federal reach and announced that the gap was constitutional.
From the meatpacking case to Plessy v. Ferguson and separate but equal is twenty-three years and four decisions, and the line through them is straight.

title: Redemption
bullets: The white South's recapture of its state governments has a name its perpetrators gave it and were proud of: Redemption, with the Redeemers cast as saviors.
The White League, formed openly in Louisiana in 1874, did not wear hoods but drilled in uniform as the avowed paramilitary arm of the Democratic Party.
On Easter Sunday 1873 a white mob with cannon attacked Black men defending the parish courthouse at Colfax, Louisiana, killing most of them after they surrendered.
The Colfax massacre, with the honest range running 60 to 100 dead, was the deadliest single act of racial violence of the entire Reconstruction era and produced zero punishment.
At Coushatta, Louisiana, in August 1874 the White League forced six white Republican officials to resign and then murdered them, signaling the counter-revolution was now total.
In September 1874 a White League force defeated the New Orleans police in the Battle of Liberty Place and held the statehouse for three days until federal troops were ordered in.
Louisiana by now had rival governors and rival legislatures, the era's worst case of contested legitimacy and the laboratory of the violence.
Redemption was a sequence, not an event: federal commitment receding and terror advancing into the vacuum in lockstep, never faster than Washington chose to stop resisting.

title: The Mississippi Plan
bullets: Mississippi in 1875 is the cleanest single proof that Reconstruction's collapse was a choice, not an inevitability; you can watch the decision get made.
Mississippi had a Black majority and was governing as a Republican state under reform governor Adelbert Ames, a Union general from Maine, honest and serious.
The Mississippi Plan had three components: economic coercion of Black sharecroppers, open paramilitary violence by rifle clubs, and deniable provocation framed as Black violence.
Rifle clubs paraded with cannon to intimidate Black communities and assassinated local leaders, with massacres at Clinton and Vicksburg killing dozens to hundreds.
Governor Ames asked President Grant for federal troops, and the Grant administration refused, the refusal put in writing.
Attorney General Edwards Pierrepont conveyed that the whole public are tired out with these annual autumnal outbreaks in the South, the well-attested calculation being the cost in Ohio.
The 1875 elections were a rout achieved by terror, the legislature flipped to the Democrats, and Ames was forced out in 1876 by a sham impeachment threat.
Mississippi became the template, refined and copied, and the literal model for the disfranchisement constitutions beginning with Mississippi's own in 1890.

title: The Bargain
bullets: By 1876 only Louisiana, South Carolina, and Florida still had Republican governments propped up by a remaining federal military presence.
South Carolina's Democrats ran the Mississippi Plan behind the Red Shirts and ex-Confederate cavalry general Wade Hampton III, fusing parades, economic threats, and murder.
The Hamburg massacre in July 1876 saw a Red Shirt force shell a Black militia armory and execute several surrendered men by name in the night; the Ellenton killings extended it.
Rutherford B. Hayes ran against Samuel Tilden, who won the national popular vote outright and sat one electoral vote short of the presidency.
Three states sent two contradictory electoral slates each, and the Constitution did not say who decides, so the country drifted toward March 4 with no president-elect.
Congress created an Electoral Commission of fifteen that, after Justice David Davis stepped off, awarded every disputed vote to Hayes on straight 8 to 7 party-line votes.
The informal Compromise of 1877, confirmed at a dinner at Washington's Wormley Hotel owned by Black Washingtonian James Wormley, traded the presidency for the abandonment of the freedpeople.
In April 1877 Hayes ordered the army to stop protecting the Louisiana and South Carolina statehouses, which collapsed within days; Tennessee had already returned Andrew Johnson to the Senate.

title: The Long Afterlife
bullets: Reconstruction was defeated, not failed, and the defeat had two parts: the destruction of Black political power and the rewriting of the story itself.
Beginning with Mississippi's new constitution in 1890, Southern states disfranchised Black men through poll taxes and literacy tests, and registration fell from majorities to near zero.
In 1896 Plessy v. Ferguson upheld separate but equal, constitutionalizing the legal order of Southern white supremacy called Jim Crow, named for a blackface minstrel character.
The United Daughters of the Confederacy promoted the Lost Cause through schools and monuments, a memory campaign whose closest parallel is the German stab-in-the-back myth.
The Dunning School, William Dunning and his students around 1900 to 1910, put a university imprimatur on the Redeemers' version of Reconstruction.
D. W. Griffith's 1915 film The Birth of a Nation dramatized the Dunning story with the Klan as heroic rescuers and directly helped rebuild a terrorist organization.
W. E. B. Du Bois demolished the lie in his 1935 Black Reconstruction in America, but the white academic mainstream dismissed rather than refuted him for thirty years.
Only around the 1960s did Kenneth Stampp, John Hope Franklin, and later Eric Foner bring the modern Second Founding consensus into the mainstream textbook.
The Fourteenth Amendment's words were wielded for sixty years overwhelmingly by corporations treated as persons, until the NAACP began reclaiming it for its original purpose.
